expanded_description: Cancer develops when genetic mutations accumulate in cells, disrupting normal growth control mechanisms. These mutations arise from various sources including inherited genetic factors, environmental exposures, and lifestyle choices. This section examines the major causes of cancer and how different factors contribute to the development of malignant disease.

core_concept.principle: Cancer results from accumulated DNA damage caused by genetic predispositions, environmental carcinogens, and lifestyle factors that overwhelm cellular repair mechanisms.
core_concept.explanation: Multiple factors contribute to cancer development. Genetic factors include inherited mutations in tumor suppressor genes or DNA repair genes. Environmental factors include exposure to carcinogens such as radiation, chemicals, and certain viruses. Lifestyle factors such as smoking, diet, and sun exposure significantly influence cancer risk. These factors cause mutations that accumulate over time, eventually leading to uncontrolled cell division.

image.alt: Diagram showing three main categories of cancer causes: genetic, environmental, and lifestyle factors
image.caption: Figure 7.9: Cancer causes are multifactorial, involving interactions between genetic susceptibility, environmental exposures, and lifestyle choices.

sections.introduction: Cancer causation is complex and typically involves multiple contributing factors acting over time. Understanding the different categories of cancer causes helps identify prevention strategies and high-risk populations. Most cancers result from a combination of genetic susceptibility and environmental exposures rather than a single cause.

key_concept.definition: Carcinogenesis is the process by which normal cells transform into cancer cells through accumulation of genetic mutations caused by various internal and external factors.
key_concept.context: Different cancer types have different predominant causes; some are primarily genetic (e.g., retinoblastoma), others primarily environmental (e.g., lung cancer from smoking), and most involve both.

image.alt: Venn diagram showing overlap between genetic, environmental, and lifestyle cancer risk factors
image.caption: Most cancers result from interactions between multiple risk factor categories.

classifications.value: Inherited or acquired mutations in genes that control cell growth, DNA repair, or apoptosis
characteristics: Present from birth or acquired during life
Affect tumor suppressor genes, oncogenes, or DNA repair genes
Can be inherited from parents or occur spontaneously
Often require multiple mutations for cancer development
classifications.behavior: Genetic mutations disrupt normal cell cycle regulation, allowing cells to divide uncontrollably or survive when they should die

examples.explanation: Mutations in BRCA1 or BRCA2 genes significantly increase risk of breast and ovarian cancer; these genes normally repair DNA damage

image.alt: BRCA gene mutation affecting DNA repair
image.caption: BRCA mutations impair DNA repair, allowing mutations to accumulate in breast and ovarian cells.

examples.explanation: Inherited mutation in APC gene causes hundreds of colon polyps, nearly guaranteeing colon cancer without intervention

image.caption: FAP demonstrates how a single inherited mutation can dramatically increase cancer risk.

examples.explanation: Inherited p53 mutation predisposes to multiple cancer types including sarcomas, breast cancer, and brain tumors

image.alt: Multiple cancer types in Li-Fraumeni syndrome
image.caption: p53 mutations affect multiple tissues, demonstrating the gene's universal role in cancer prevention.

classifications.value: External agents that damage DNA and increase cancer risk through direct mutation or chronic inflammation
characteristics: Include chemicals, radiation, and biological agents
Exposure may be occupational, residential, or recreational
Effect depends on dose, duration, and timing of exposure
Often act synergistically with genetic factors
classifications.behavior: Carcinogens cause DNA damage directly (mutagens) or indirectly (through inflammation or hormone disruption), leading to mutations that can initiate cancer

examples.explanation: X-rays, gamma rays, and radioactive materials cause DNA double-strand breaks; exposure from medical imaging, radon gas, or nuclear accidents increases cancer risk

image.caption: Ionizing radiation directly damages DNA, potentially causing mutations that lead to cancer.

examples.explanation: Substances like asbestos, benzene, formaldehyde, and aflatoxin cause DNA damage; exposure may be occupational or through contaminated food/water

image.alt: Common chemical carcinogens and their sources
image.caption: Chemical carcinogens are found in various occupational and environmental settings.

examples.explanation: HPV (cervical cancer), HBV/HCV (liver cancer), EBV (lymphomas), and HTLV-1 (leukemia) introduce viral genes that disrupt cell cycle control

image.caption: Certain viruses contribute to approximately 15-20% of human cancers worldwide.

classifications.value: Behavioral choices that influence cancer risk through exposure to carcinogens or effects on metabolism and immunity
characteristics: Include tobacco use, diet, alcohol, physical activity, and sun exposure
classifications.behavior: Lifestyle factors expose cells to carcinogens, alter hormone levels, affect immune function, or cause chronic inflammation that promotes cancer development

examples.explanation: Cigarette smoke contains over 70 known carcinogens; smoking causes approximately 30% of all cancer deaths including lung, bladder, and pancreatic cancers

image.caption: Smoking is the single largest preventable cause of cancer worldwide.

examples.explanation: Sunlight and tanning beds emit UV radiation that damages skin cell DNA; major cause of basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma

image.alt: UV radiation causing DNA damage in skin cells
image.caption: UV radiation causes characteristic DNA mutations (thymine dimers) that can lead to skin cancer.

examples.explanation: High-fat, low-fiber diets and obesity increase risk of colon, breast, and other cancers through inflammation, hormone changes, and metabolic effects

image.caption: Diet influences cancer risk through multiple mechanisms including inflammation and hormone regulation.

principles.statement: Cancer development typically requires multiple genetic mutations (hits) accumulated over time, not a single event.
principles.application: Explains why cancer risk increases with age and why exposure to multiple carcinogens increases risk more than single exposures.
principles.example: Colon cancer typically requires 5-7 mutations in different genes (APC, KRAS, p53, etc.) before becoming malignant.
principles.analogy: Like a car needing multiple brake failures before a crash—one failed brake is concerning, but multiple failures make an accident nearly inevitable.

image.alt: Diagram showing accumulation of mutations leading to cancer
image.caption: The multi-hit model explains why cancer typically develops over many years.

principles.statement: Cancer risk generally increases with the amount and duration of exposure to carcinogens.
principles.application: Forms the basis for exposure limits and safety regulations; supports smoking cessation and reduced carcinogen exposure.
principles.example: Lung cancer risk increases with pack-years of smoking (packs per day × years smoked); heavy smokers have much higher risk than light smokers.
principles.analogy: Like sunburn—brief exposure causes little damage, but prolonged exposure causes severe burns and increases skin cancer risk.

image.alt: Graph showing cancer risk increasing with carcinogen dose
image.caption: Higher and longer exposures to carcinogens generally produce higher cancer risks.

principles.statement: There is typically a long delay (years to decades) between carcinogen exposure and cancer development.
principles.application: Explains why cancers appear in older adults even when exposures occurred in youth; emphasizes importance of early prevention.
principles.example: Asbestos exposure in young workers may not cause mesothelioma until 20-40 years later.
principles.analogy: Like planting a tree seed—the tree doesn't appear immediately but grows slowly over many years before becoming visible.

image.alt: Timeline showing exposure, latency, and cancer development
image.caption: The latency period between exposure and cancer can span decades.

principles.statement: Genetic susceptibility and environmental exposures interact to determine individual cancer risk.
principles.application: Explains why not everyone exposed to a carcinogen develops cancer; supports personalized prevention strategies.
principles.example: Smokers with certain genetic variants in carcinogen-metabolizing enzymes have much higher lung cancer risk than other smokers.
principles.analogy: Like a lock and key—environmental exposures are the key, but genetic makeup determines whether the lock opens easily.

image.alt: Interaction between genetic and environmental factors
image.caption: Both genes and environment contribute to cancer risk, often interacting synergistically.

components.definition: Carcinogenesis involves multiple molecular components including DNA damage, mutation accumulation, and disruption of cellular control mechanisms.
components.interpretation: Cancer risk is determined by the interaction of genetic factors, environmental exposures, duration of exposure, and the cell's ability to repair damage. Higher susceptibility, exposure, or time increases risk; better repair capacity decreases risk.

image.alt: Molecular components involved in cancer development
image.caption: Carcinogenesis involves DNA damage, mutation fixation, clonal expansion, and tumor progression.

categories.description: Physical agents that cause cancer through direct DNA damage or tissue injury
categories.detailed_mechanism: Ionizing radiation causes DNA strand breaks and chromosomal abnormalities; UV radiation causes thymine dimers; chronic tissue injury causes inflammation that promotes cancer

examples.explanation: Includes X-rays, gamma rays, radon gas, and radioactive materials; causes direct DNA damage and chromosomal breaks; medical imaging, occupational exposure, and environmental radon are sources

image.alt: Sources and effects of ionizing radiation
image.caption: Ionizing radiation is a proven human carcinogen affecting multiple organ systems.

examples.explanation: From sunlight and tanning beds; causes thymine dimers in DNA; major cause of skin cancers including melanoma; risk increased by fair skin, many moles, and sunburn history

image.alt: UV radiation effects on skin cell DNA
image.caption: UV radiation is the primary cause of preventable skin cancer.
Physical carcinogens damage DNA through energy transfer rather than chemical interaction.

categories.description: Chemical substances that cause cancer through direct DNA damage or metabolic activation
categories.detailed_mechanism: Some chemicals directly damage DNA (direct-acting); others require metabolic activation by liver enzymes to become carcinogenic (procarcinogens); many form DNA adducts that cause mutations during replication

examples.explanation: Contains polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), nitrosamines, and benzene; causes DNA adducts in lung, bladder, and other tissues; responsible for multiple cancer types

image.caption: Tobacco smoke contains over 70 known carcinogenic chemicals.

examples.explanation: Toxin produced by Aspergillus mold on stored grains and nuts; causes specific p53 mutations; major cause of liver cancer in regions with poor food storage

image.caption: Aflatoxin exposure is a major preventable cause of liver cancer worldwide.

examples.explanation: Fibrous mineral used in construction; fibers cause chronic inflammation and DNA damage in lung lining; causes mesothelioma and lung cancer with 20-40 year latency

image.caption: Asbestos exposure causes mesothelioma, a cancer of the lung lining.

image.alt: Major chemical carcinogens and their sources
image.caption: Chemical carcinogens are found in tobacco, food, occupational settings, and the environment.

categories.description: Living organisms or their products that cause cancer through infection or toxin production
categories.detailed_mechanism: Oncogenic viruses insert viral genes that disrupt cell cycle control; bacteria cause chronic inflammation; parasites cause tissue damage and inflammation that promote cancer

examples.explanation: Sexually transmitted virus; high-risk types (16, 18) produce E6 and E7 proteins that inactivate p53 and Rb; causes cervical, anal, and oropharyngeal cancers; preventable by vaccination

image.alt: HPV infection leading to cervical cancer
image.caption: HPV vaccination can prevent most cervical cancers.

examples.explanation: Blood-borne viruses causing chronic liver infection; chronic inflammation and viral proteins promote liver cancer; major cause of hepatocellular carcinoma worldwide

image.alt: Hepatitis infection leading to liver cancer
image.caption: Chronic hepatitis infection is a leading cause of liver cancer globally.

examples.explanation: Bacteria causing chronic stomach infection and inflammation; increases risk of gastric cancer and MALT lymphoma; treatable with antibiotics

image.alt: H. pylori infection and gastric cancer
image.caption: H. pylori is the only bacterium classified as a human carcinogen.
Biological carcinogens account for approximately 15-20% of cancers worldwide.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Relative Cancer Risk from Smoking
worked_examples.problem: If a non-smoker has a baseline lung cancer risk of 1%, and smoking increases risk 25-fold, what is the lung cancer risk for a smoker? If someone smokes 2 packs per day for 30 years, what is their pack-year history?

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate the smoker's risk by multiplying baseline risk by the relative risk increase.
Calculate pack-years by multiplying packs per day by years smoked.
Interpret the results: smoking dramatically increases absolute risk, and pack-years quantify cumulative exposure.

worked_examples.final_answer: A smoker has 25% lung cancer risk (vs 1% for non-smoker). The person has a 60 pack-year smoking history, indicating very high cumulative exposure.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Cancer risk increases with cumulative exposure to carcinogens; pack-years quantify smoking exposure and correlate with cancer risk.

image.alt: Graph showing lung cancer risk by pack-years
image.caption: Lung cancer risk increases progressively with pack-year smoking history.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Cancer Risk Factors from Patient History
worked_examples.problem: A 55-year-old patient presents with the following history: smoker for 35 years (1 pack/day), works in construction with asbestos exposure, father had colon cancer at age 50, drinks alcohol daily, and has chronic hepatitis C. Identify the cancer risk factors and potential cancer types.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify each risk factor from the history: smoking, asbestos, family history, alcohol, hepatitis C.
Match each risk factor to associated cancer types based on known carcinogen-cancer relationships.
Consider synergistic effects: smoking + asbestos multiplies lung cancer risk; hepatitis C + alcohol multiplies liver cancer risk.

worked_examples.final_answer: Risk factors: smoking (lung, bladder, pancreatic cancer), asbestos (mesothelioma, lung cancer), family history (colon cancer), alcohol (liver, esophageal cancer), hepatitis C (liver cancer). Synergistic effects significantly increase lung and liver cancer risk.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Multiple risk factors can act synergistically; comprehensive risk assessment considers all exposures and their interactions.

image.caption: Comprehensive cancer risk assessment considers all exposures and their interactions.

components.composition: Inherited mutations in tumor suppressor genes, oncogenes, or DNA repair genes
components.function: Determines baseline vulnerability to cancer development; affects ability to repair DNA damage
components.key_feature: Present from birth; affects all cells in body; may require additional environmental triggers

image.caption: Inherited mutations provide the first 'hit' in the multi-hit model of cancer.

components.composition: Physical, chemical, and biological carcinogens encountered in daily life
components.function: Cause DNA damage that can initiate cancer if not repaired; effects accumulate over time
components.key_feature: Often modifiable through behavior change or regulation; dose and duration determine risk

image.caption: Environmental exposures are major contributors to preventable cancers.

components.composition: Behavioral choices affecting carcinogen exposure, metabolism, and immune function
components.function: Influence cancer risk through direct carcinogen exposure, hormone modulation, inflammation, and immune surveillance
components.key_feature: Modifiable through individual choices; prevention potential is high

image.caption: Lifestyle modifications can significantly reduce cancer risk.

function.operation: Cancer causes act through DNA damage, mutation accumulation, and disruption of cellular control mechanisms that normally prevent uncontrolled growth.
function.energy: DNA repair processes require ATP; carcinogen metabolism may consume cellular energy; chronic inflammation increases metabolic demand.
function.mechanism: Carcinogens cause DNA damage directly or through metabolic activation; mutations accumulate when damage exceeds repair capacity; selected clones expand to form tumors.

image.alt: Functional pathways from carcinogen exposure to cancer
image.caption: Multiple pathways connect carcinogen exposure to cancer development.

cycle.operation: Carcinogen causes irreversible DNA mutation in a single cell
cycle.purpose: Creates the first step in cancer development; initiated cell has growth advantage

image.caption: Initiation is the first irreversible step in carcinogenesis.

cycle.operation: Additional factors stimulate proliferation of initiated cells without causing new mutations
cycle.purpose: Expands the population of initiated cells, increasing probability of additional mutations

image.caption: Promotion expands initiated cell populations, facilitating further mutation accumulation.

cycle.operation: Additional mutations confer invasive and metastatic capabilities
cycle.purpose: Completes transformation to malignant cancer with ability to spread

image.caption: Progression completes the transformation from normal cell to malignant cancer.

process_flow.description: Cell is exposed to physical, chemical, or biological carcinogen through various routes (inhalation, ingestion, skin contact, infection).
process_flow.mechanism: Carcinogen enters body and reaches target tissue; may require metabolic activation by liver enzymes to become reactive.
process_flow.result: Carcinogen interacts with cellular components, particularly DNA, causing damage that may become permanent mutation.

image.caption: Carcinogens enter the body through multiple routes depending on the agent.

process_flow.description: Carcinogen causes structural damage to DNA including strand breaks, base modifications, or crosslinks.
process_flow.mechanism: Direct-acting carcinogens bind to DNA forming adducts; indirect carcinogens are metabolized to reactive intermediates that damage DNA; radiation causes strand breaks.
process_flow.result: DNA damage triggers cellular response including cell cycle arrest and DNA repair; if damage is severe, apoptosis may occur.

image.alt: Types of DNA damage from carcinogens
image.caption: Different carcinogens cause different types of DNA damage.

process_flow.description: If DNA damage is not repaired before cell division, the damage becomes a permanent mutation in daughter cells.
process_flow.mechanism: DNA polymerase replicates damaged DNA incorrectly; mismatch repair may fail; mutation is incorporated into genome of both daughter cells.
process_flow.result: Permanent genetic change that may affect genes controlling cell growth, DNA repair, or apoptosis.

image.caption: Unrepaired DNA damage becomes fixed as permanent mutations during cell division.

process_flow.description: Cells with growth-advantage mutations proliferate more than normal cells, forming a clone of mutated cells.
process_flow.mechanism: Mutations in oncogenes or tumor suppressors provide selective advantage; clone expands through repeated cell divisions; additional mutations accumulate in expanding population.
process_flow.result: Population of cells with multiple mutations; may form benign tumor or progress to malignancy.

image.caption: Clonal expansion increases the population at risk for additional mutations.

process_flow.description: Accumulation of sufficient mutations confers invasive and metastatic capabilities, completing transformation to cancer.
process_flow.mechanism: Mutations affect genes controlling invasion (MMPs), angiogenesis (VEGF), immune evasion (PD-L1), and metastasis; tumor microenvironment supports malignant growth.
process_flow.result: Fully malignant cancer capable of local invasion and distant metastasis; clinical disease becomes apparent.

image.caption: Malignant transformation completes the carcinogenesis process.

process_types.energy_profile: Prevention requires behavioral and environmental modifications; direction: risk reduction through intervention
process_types.direction: Modifiable factors can be reduced; non-modifiable factors require monitoring and early detection
process_types.function: Distinguishes cancer causes that can be prevented from those that cannot, guiding prevention strategies

examples.explanation: Tobacco use (30% of cancers), UV exposure (most skin cancers), certain infections (HPV, hepatitis), occupational exposures, alcohol, diet and obesity; estimated 40-50% of cancers are preventable

image.alt: Preventable cancer causes and their contribution
image.caption: A substantial proportion of cancers are preventable through lifestyle and environmental modifications.

examples.explanation: Inherited genetic mutations, aging (accumulated mutations over time), random replication errors, some environmental exposures that cannot be avoided; require early detection and monitoring

image.caption: Non-preventable factors require different management strategies focused on early detection.

image.alt: Comparison of preventable and non-preventable cancer causes
image.caption: Understanding which causes are preventable guides public health and individual prevention efforts.

process_types.energy_profile: Risk increases with age due to accumulated mutations; direction: progressive increase over lifetime
process_types.direction: Cancer incidence increases exponentially with age in most cancer types
process_types.function: Explains why cancer is primarily a disease of older adults despite exposures occurring throughout life

examples.explanation: Rare (<1% of cancers); often involve inherited mutations or developmental errors; different types than adult cancers (leukemias, brain tumors, sarcomas)

image.caption: Childhood cancers have different causes and types than adult cancers.

examples.explanation: Incidence increases dramatically after age 50; reflects accumulated mutations over decades; most common cancers (breast, prostate, colon, lung) are age-related

image.caption: Cancer incidence increases exponentially with age in most cancer types.
Cancer risk increases with age due to accumulated mutations and declining repair capacity.

sections.definition: Cancer causes are factors that contribute to the development of malignant disease through DNA damage, mutation accumulation, and disruption of cellular control mechanisms.
sections.purpose: To understand the various genetic, environmental, and lifestyle factors that cause cancer, enabling prevention strategies, risk assessment, and early detection approaches.

activation.definition: Carcinogenesis is activated when DNA damage exceeds cellular repair capacity, allowing mutations to accumulate in genes controlling cell growth and survival.
activation.effect: Initiated cells gain selective advantage, expand clonally, and acquire additional mutations that progressively increase malignant potential.

image.alt: Activation of carcinogenesis through mutation accumulation
image.caption: Carcinogenesis is activated when mutations accumulate beyond repair capacity.

key_properties.explanation: Multiple risk factors often interact synergistically, producing greater risk than the sum of individual factors; smoking and asbestos together increase lung cancer risk 50-60 fold (vs 25-fold for smoking alone).

image.alt: Synergistic interaction of cancer risk factors
image.caption: Combined risk factors can produce multiplicative rather than additive effects.

key_properties.explanation: Genetic variations in carcinogen metabolism, DNA repair capacity, and immune function cause wide variation in individual cancer risk even with similar exposures.

image.caption: Genetic differences explain why not everyone exposed to carcinogens develops cancer.

key_properties.explanation: An estimated 40-50% of cancers are preventable through modification of known risk factors including tobacco cessation, vaccination, sun protection, healthy diet, and reduced alcohol consumption.

image.caption: A substantial proportion of cancers could be prevented through known interventions.

key_properties.explanation: Long latency periods (often 20-40 years) between exposure and cancer development mean that prevention efforts in youth may not show benefits until decades later.

image.alt: Latency period between exposure and cancer
image.caption: Long latency periods emphasize the importance of early prevention.

summary: Cancer causes are multifactorial, involving genetic susceptibility, environmental exposures, and lifestyle choices that interact to produce DNA damage and mutation accumulation. Genetic factors include inherited mutations in tumor suppressor genes (BRCA, p53, APC) that predispose to specific cancers. Environmental carcinogens include physical agents (ionizing and UV radiation), chemical agents (tobacco smoke, asbestos, aflatoxin), and biological agents (HPV, hepatitis viruses, H. pylori). Lifestyle factors such as smoking, alcohol consumption, diet, obesity, and sun exposure significantly influence cancer risk and are largely modifiable. Cancer development follows the multi-hit hypothesis requiring multiple mutations accumulated over time, explaining why risk increases with age and why there is often a long latency period (20-40 years) between exposure and cancer. Risk factors often act synergistically, with combined exposures producing greater risk than individual factors alone. An estimated 40-50% of cancers are preventable through modification of known risk factors. Understanding cancer causes enables prevention strategies, risk assessment, early detection programs, and public health interventions to reduce cancer burden. Cancer treatments that target specific causes and mutations are discussed in Section 7.10.
